Beveridge traced the Haidarabad Codex, found and multiplied it, clarified confusion that had made the Bukhara Babur-nama mistaken for a reproduction of Babur's true text, led to its preservation in facsimile, and it became the first item in the Memorial of literary work done in the name of the Turkish scholar, safeguarded in hundred-fold facsimile.
The subject was given Balkh, and a few years after his father's death he died; Khanzada Begim was in Merv when Shah Ismail defeated the Auzbegs, and he escorted her to Qunduz where she rejoined him.

The subject's second battle was on the Urus in Turkistan against Auzbegs returning from a raid near Samarkand; he crossed the river on ice, defeated them, took prisoners and booty, but returned everything to its owners.

The subject, being just, sent overseers to help a snow-affected caravan from Khitai in Eastern Andijan, and after a year or two, he transferred all the caravan's property to its heirs from Khurasan and Samarkand.

After a second conquest of Samarkand, the subject held it for five months but left it due to lack of support; during that time Khanzada Begim fell to Muhammad Shaibani Khan and had a son, Khurram Shah.

The subject had three sons—Zahiruddin Muhammad Babur (eldest, mother Qutluq-nigar Khanim), Jahangir Mirza (second, mother Fatima-sultan, a Mughal tuman-beg), and Nasir Mirza (third, mother Umid, an Andijani mistress)—and five daughters, including Khanzada Begim, his eldest daughter and full sister, five years older than him.
